sentences.text: My mother and my sister that is older from me two years.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Gershon is--
--a brother that he was two or three years younger.
I don't know.
Hershl and me-- five people.
Yeah.
No.
We--
I beg your pardon?
No.
We saw in the beginning-- when we-- the way this was handled-- took us on trucks, not prepare the people to walk up and sit down.
Like they said, you are going to work.
And-- and then we got to do the-- where the cargo was there-- the-- the-- the-- the train or whatever, and this is-- the openings of those trains is very high-- the floor.
It's this high.
All people-- woman, the heavy people-- couldn't walk up, couldn't jump up to here and then pull yourself in.
And at the same time, you had the whole length-- I don't know how many-- this-- what do you call this?
Each segment-- each-- each--
The cars.
This cars-- those cars, when you didn't see an end of it-- and everybody-- old women, people-- they cannot go in-- nothing to walk on to go on.
And they're screaming.
And the Germans screaming the whole length-- you [GERMAN], you-- you-- you [GERMAN] with this, with that, you know, all kind of this.
They make you right away low, nothing.
You're not worth anything.
So what happened was that we had to pull everybody up and help each other.
And they filled it up.
They didn't-- in those cargo-- they filled it up with I don't know how many people.
You were sitting, like-- packed each other close.
You know, and then they locked it-- locked it, and it was just an opening there with a little screen on top in both ends.
And I'll tell you something.
Excuse me, you were making a [INAUDIBLE] in this-- in this place, peeing in the place on each other.
You turned in not a human anymore.
That's it.
You saw you were going for something terrible.
Nothing.
What I was wearing.
I don't know.
I don't know what it was.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yes, I mean, yeah.
I-- I don't know.
You know, you were sitting inside in the dark, and nothing-- a little light on top there.
They had-- they stop once in a few times.
So one time, they throw in a herring-- herring.
We didn't have for days a little water to drink, and they give you herrings to eat-- schmaltz herring, you know, salty.
How many died?
A fortune of people died in the-- in those-- in those trains.
This--
Daytime.
No, but I saw that it's a camp with the-- with the-- like the ghetto-- the walls--
I saw.
Oh, terrible thing-- terrible.
What happened is they opened up those doors, sliding doors-- both sides-- one here, one there.
And they start screaming-- this was not Germans, again, Jewish.
And they were working over there-- [SPEAKING GERMAN].
I-- thought it's Germans.
I thought it German-- [GERMAN]!
[SPEAKING GERMAN]!
Old women-- my mother was-- was-- was-- was hardly move.
And this what happened-- everybody down who couldn't walk, who was dead-- they left, and they cleaned it up.
And-- and everything and over there where the train stopped was asphalt.
And everybody stood-- fives, tens, you know, but they were packed together along-- the way the train-- this one.
I thought all the time that those people that they say, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
I learned later-- [INAUDIBLE].
Normal clothes with the-- the [INAUDIBLE], you know.
Yeah.
Oh, Germans stood on both sides--
--with dogs--
--German Shepherds, with other.
And the line-- and in the front of the line was the officers-- big shots-- and they select, this side, this side, this side, this side.
And my mother says to me, Zalman, [SPEAKING GERMAN] Oh, forgot.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
Bigger, bigger, bigger, to show that I'm not 13 years old-- nothing to do with you and this saved me.
And my sister too.
Yeah, no, no, we together down, and the sister went one way, I went another way.
And my mother with the two kids-- she went in the crematorium.
I didn't know where we should go.
I was going into one of the barracks-- not right away.
We-- we-- we stood in an area there.
And over there was people that watch out.
And then your stood in line, and they sheared the hair.
They took the hair off.
And they-- and they-- and they-- they shaved you down here.
And this shaving felt [? murder. ?]
Because that-- I don't know what they had-- blades no blades.
And the guy grabbed you like-- like you were a wild man, and you cut and bleeded and-- and this.
And this is not [? chop. ?] One after another he does this, you know.
They didn't care, nothing.
A Jew again.
No, you have to do it.
But as a human being, behave nice.
They knock your teeth out.
You were afraid.
You cried, you this.
And then they put you in to take a shower.
So packed a-- double or triple the amount that can-- they could shower.
And they put it in, and they put hot boiled water-- you can make the hardest stuff soft-- boiled water.
So you start running to the walls.
Here, the two guys going around in those wooden shoes with a stick-- a wooden stick.
And he's over there-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] you know, you're dirty, you're filthy, and this and that.
You don't want to wash.
[SPEAKING GERMAN], ice cold.
You couldn't go in there-- ice cold, ice!
You know, so this is what we went through, and then they put us in-- in a-- in a barrack, filled up the barrack.
Not yet, naked.
Now you go into the barrack, and a guy comes, you know, and the-- those barracks in Auschwitz used to be a Polish army camp.
Five or six, I don't know.
Oh, I think one or two.
I don't remember.
Who-- who was remembering a number?
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so this was-- they used to keep horses in it.
So you had the place where they used to eat the horses-- in both sides, horses, you know?
And this place-- they used to fill it up with food.
So what they did now, they put a piece of wood on top, and they closed it.
And on both sides, people were there.
And we were sitting this close-- each other close-- all the way till this.
So many people they packed in.
We were sitting and waiting.
Then comes in a guy.
He goes on top of this.
They're sticking their hands, you know, to put it up here.
And he holds it, and he said, who wants to live?
Who wants to live?
You better give me the gold.
Give me the-- the diamonds, what you hide it, you swallow it, whatever.
You give it because you're gonna be fried.
You give the gold if you have it, and tell me where it is.
Some people did.
Did you have gold?
I didn't have no gold.
Nothing.
He-- he speaks generally to everybody.
But some people had.
After he passed the whole thing, I knew there were guys that had gold inside they swallowed.
Swallowed-- they-- one guy had two diamonds-- nice big ones-- swallowed.
He said, I'm gonna die anyway.
Why to give them?
So and after this, we went out.
They put us in-- in the blocks.
They give out the clothes.
But they took off before-- before they went in the crematorium-- all those clothes, you know.
So they give one-- he-- he needs that long, and he gets this long.
You know?
No underwear, no nothing, no shoes-- bare feet till we got some shoes later on they gave.
Then you couldn't walk it.
It's too small, too-- one is this, and one is this.
Yeah.
They'd stand [? there. ?] Some people killed themselves.
Some people could throw themselves on the wires of the-- of the-- of the fence.
Right away.
Yeah.
Here.
A Jewish guy.
Every Jew is a-- a-- a--
A political prisoner.
Yeah, the sign.
The sign of a political prisoner.
OK.
How long did you stay in that barrack?
I don't know.
Six months, seven months.
Yeah.
Well, in the beginning, they-- they put out to clean the areas, digging for people that coming out from the crematorium-- holes, you know.
To bury them.
As bodies.
Bodies, bodies.
They-- they-- then later on, you get a little know the guy that managed in-- in the group that you work.
So you get-- and then I worked the cleaning a little-- it was a-- was a-- over there, they would made bricks to-- they make bricks.
So I worked there a little bit cleaning, bringing sand, doing this, this with everybody had to work.
But later on, I got-- the first time, I got closer to the crematoriums.
And the crematoriums-- the guys that were working-- they-- they couldn't-- nobody could talk to them because this was an isolated area.
The other guys?
Oh, the guys that shoveled in the people?
Oh, this is what they do-- they put them on this--
--this ledge.
And then the sliding in--
They were taking the bodies out-- out of the crematorium?
No, this-- the crematorium-- they were burned there.
They lit all that was left.
The bodies?
There was a mountain two stories high with dead people.
And they were taking these bodies--
They die, yeah.
If then they didn't die, they poisoned them-- that's it-- to die.
They put them in the gas chamber.
They had-- the-- the-- the gas chamber was so full, they couldn't make it.
So they poisoned people by drinking something.
Burned them.
They couldn't burn them that much.
You know, what they had there before-- when I left Auschwitz, I think they had, three stories high, a mountain of people.
So I-- I, you know-- you wanted to go to work because you knew, if you stay in the barrack, or you're not going, you're gone.
They used to pick up every day people they needed to burn.
You better go to work and better not being sick.
Sick people used to go till they couldn't walk anymore.
Yeah.
So what happened then is, they took me and other people to clean around the-- the gas chamber over there, not to have signs that people get the uprising before to see what's happening.
But they [INAUDIBLE] at the end of the buildings, you know, no matter what-- always was filthy dirt from the people.
They-- they're screaming, and they're clawing.
They see they're going to dead-- to die.
So this is what we used to clean.
You had this road-- the-- the-- the wagons on two wheels-- they did-- with one wheel.
You lift it up, and the whole thing folds up, you know?
So we used to be close to this.
We have the ashes stay out.
Ashes of people, everything.
Used to fill up ashes they used to throw out.
If I'm not-- they used to-- this was hidden, but they used to have-- I believe they used to have their people-- farmers-- coming in the back.
There was a ditch there lower down.
Over there was ashes galore.
They didn't know where to put it.
They tried to dig holes to dig it to cover this, but they used it for farms.
Fertilizer.
But when you had the wagon with the ashes--
We--
In the hole, in-- in a ditch, all the dirt around it.
Sure.
Oh, quite a few people.
I don't know.
A month, month and a half.
I went away.
No, no, I went away already.
I went away to the other camps.
Transferred to Dachau.
No, no.
No, no, they-- they-- each time, when you stood in counting.
They're counting.
Every morning.
They count every morning-- who everybody's at work, the people that working that day.
So this-- so they used to-- sometime every week or every two weeks-- asking for professionals-- who was a shoemaker, who was a barber, who was a everything, you know.
So they came out [INAUDIBLE] [? die ?] maker So I lift up a hand, and I stood on my feet.
I stood-- oh, oh, my god.
I don't-- I forgot.
That doesn't work.
It's going to be in it.
[LAUGHTER] Sorry, I almost ripped it.
So, so I-- I-- I-- I stood up here, you know, automatic, without thinking, lifting up the hand.
[POINTING] But that's a little different.
This is a photographer.
[LAUGHS]
So you know, I'm smiling with my tears.
So anyway, so I lifted up, and I stood on my toes to see that I'm big man, you know.
I'm not a little boy.
So they-- everybody-- you're out, out, out, out, out, out.
And I went to Dachau.
The trucks.
I don't know.
Going, and going-- I don't know, 10 days, five days.
I don't know, you're--
Sit packed in.
When-- they put you in a-- in a block.
I don't remember either.
No, no, no, no, no.
It was-- was-- it was about 50 people in-- I think 40 or 50 people in the barrack.
And the barrack was built in such a way that the roof goes down into the earth.
And grass grows on it.
Not the roof there, but it's not [INAUDIBLE] so that it's flat.
It was a lot of-- this place is big.
And-- and you walked in lower.
You know?
And there was wooden-- the whole length with wood.
I think it was maybe 50 people in the barrack.
50 people.
Yeah, they're laying like-- like this, you know.
And just wood.
There used to be a little hay on the-- on this.
Oh, yeah, there was a kapo.
Mhm.
There was a blockalteste and a kapo.
The blockalteste there was the guy who was responsible with this group that is in this-- in this room.
Yes.
He was good, this one.
He tried to help.
He didn't kick.
He didn't hit.
He just hit-- sometime he gave a kick, you know, but the kick is not the kick that kills you.
Some would give you a kick that you couldn't sit already for weeks.
Yeah, but the kapo-- the same thing.
Some were human.
If the Germans were stood, he make believe that he hits, that he's good because he wanted to have this job.
Yes, they all Jewish.
But what they did-- their trick was too [? easy ?] the Germans.
They're mixed.
If it's a new guy in a group of people Jewish, they take a Polack or a Czech.
And if it's a Romanian, he takes a Hungarian to be a kapo.
So for some reason, this makes a little hate between if you didn't listen to him right away and you didn't do the right way.
So he makes in front of the Germans that he is good.
He is good, he is with them.
So they liked him, so he had the job.
No.
The only way if the-- mine-- mine-- this was a blockalteste there.
He loved me.
He liked me very much.
He saw-- he saw in me a little bit of-- I'm more a little a mensch with feelings, you know?
I was talking to him when he got a little-- I felt I have no afraid anymore for him that he's going to do something to me.
And he listened.
And-- and then I had a friend over there.
Not this one.
This is after-- I had-- before-- this mine-- mine that I got with this one to do.
Every morning, before you went to work, you go out in the field for the-- an appell.
In German is an appell, where you stayed up.
And every barrack were stood-- stood there with their-- with their blockalteste, with this guy.
And he stood in the front.
And this is a few thousand people like.
And the German officer comes and counts if everybody is there.
And each blockalteste goes after this German.
He finished his line, the blockalteste stays with his part, and the German continues to another group of people, and so the other blockalteste.
Now, this friend that I was friendly with him, he got sick.
Every morning, when we left, nobody could-- every morning, when we went to work, the Germans checks the barracks.
They used to check it if nobody hide or do something.
So he got so sick, so every time, when you're stood in the line, sometime a doctor, if he was a doctor-- the German-- to look to see if nobody's sick with a disease.
They were afraid because they have the army-- the soldiers-- right outside the camp by their camp.
So they're afraid to have anything to do with us.
So they watched.
If nobody's anything [INAUDIBLE] sick, [BACKROUND NOISE] so they used to check just-- they used to tell everybody to open this, you know, to check-- oh-- to check it if he is not sick.
So I went to him-- this blockalteste-- and I said, listen, he-- he cannot walk, and he is filled with [INAUDIBLE].
You know what [INAUDIBLE] is?
He's filled with [INAUDIBLE].
And I don't want him to go.
You know, [INAUDIBLE] with guys, a head and a foot, and [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
And that's it-- goes in the crematorium.
So but there was not crematorium, so they would put them in the-- in this-- they had ditches with the bulldozers, builded up.
And the people with the wagons they used to carry the deaths out there and lay them out like herrings, you know.
And that's all.
So I said, listen, I don't want him to die.
So I get [INAUDIBLE].
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
And he didn't say anything.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
And he is telling me, if he doesn't have [INAUDIBLE], [SPEAKING GERMAN], this, a nail, had something-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], that he passes.
He sees it's nice and clean, you know, pass.
He couldn't walk.
And when he passed by, he saw something's wrong.
He calls right away two guys, put him on the truck.
They put me-- they put me in-- in a-- in a [INAUDIBLE] [? die maker. ?] Half of this place was Polacks working-- all kinds of Polacks.
Half was Jewish.
And-- and-- and they put me in the milling machine.
They give me the [INAUDIBLE].
The German-- the meister-- he was limping on a foot with a stick.
He was walking with a cane.
When I came in, I heard screaming.
I didn't know that this is here.
What a [INAUDIBLE] this is.
And you murdered wild men-- old men-- he didn't have teeth in the mouth.
But he was a-- an [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He would eat a Jew up.
And [LAUGHS] I stood this near machine.
A guy brought over the iron with the-- with the little wagon, you know, little thing, puts down-- puts down the-- the-- the-- the plan of what to do and walked away.
This is yours.
Near me was a Jewish guy working.
He was-- I don't know-- 50, 55, 52.
And he-- I saw he works.
Boy, oh boy.
I knew to put the machine on, you know, looked a little here, there, there, there, there.
When my father was working, it was an old machine.
This was a better machine-- German machine-- so a milling machine.
And I knew this guy is going to come.
The guy is going to check it.
I didn't know about this [INAUDIBLE]-- this-- this guy.
So I went over to this guy, and I say, you know, help me.
I don't know what to do here.
I didn't know what to do.
So he-- he says to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
He took out his [? ready ?] part.
He put this in my machine.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he said, [GERMAN]-- leave the machine working, and have the cutting on the spot.
You see him coming in the back, make believe you start cutting, and this, put the machine in place, you know, like this.
And when he goes to you, you see him and say, [GERMAN]-- I finished your piece!
You know?
And this what I did.
And he comes to me, and he says, finished?
Yeah, finished.
See?
Good.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
He-- in mean time, another one-- the Jewish guy.
We're going to--
I was two months like this.
You know, two months I was with this guy.
